prompt:
Hi — I keep the software for our beekeeping club (it's one Raspberry Pi and a lot of enthusiasm). Inspection sheets get transcribed into pipe-delimited lines, one per hive visit, like `A7|2026-03-16|6|Q|supered` — hive code, date, brood frame count, Q if the queen was sighted or - if not, then a free-text note (can be empty, can have commas and spaces, never a pipe). Exactly five fields.

I want a proper Ruby collection over these lines so the report scripts stop hand-splitting strings: `InspectionLog` in `inspection_log.rb`, constructed with the array of raw lines.

The deal is: `each` is the source of truth. It parses ONE line at a time, on demand, and yields a hash per visit: `{ hive: "A7", date: "2026-03-16", frames: 6, queen: true, note: "supered" }` — frames as an Integer, queen as true/false. Construction must never parse anything (I sometimes load files with a known-bad tail and only read the top). With a block, `each` returns the log itself, like Array#each returns the array. Without a block it returns an Enumerator that works externally — `.next`, StopIteration when it runs dry, the whole protocol.

Include Enumerable and don't fight it: map, select, reduce, count (with and without a block), min_by, first, include?, sort_by — all just work, exactly like they would on an array of those hashes. Same for the index variants: `each_with_index`, and `each.with_index(1)` when the report wants 1-based numbering.

A line that's wrong — not five fields, frames not all digits, queen flag anything but Q or - — raises ArgumentError with message exactly `bad inspection line 3: <the raw line>` (1-based position in the input array). But ONLY when iteration actually reaches that line: `first(3)` over a log whose 4th line is garbage must succeed.

And for the big seasonal files there's `scan`: returns an Enumerator::Lazy over the parsed rows, so I can do `log.scan.select { ... }.first(5)` and it parses only as far as it needs — if the answer is in the first two lines, line three is never even looked at. That short-circuiting is tested with probe counters, not vibes.

Empty log = empty collection (to_a is [], first is nil, count is 0, each.next raises StopIteration immediately).

Everything is pinned in `test_inspection_log.rb` (leave it alone). Green means: `ruby test_inspection_log.rb`.
